items.prompt_md: In the brute-force pair-check above, why is the runtime **O(n²)** rather than O(n)?
options: Because the inner loop is nested inside the outer loop, so comparisons scale with n * n
Because dict lookups are O(n) each
Because sorting the array first takes O(n log n)
Because the array might contain duplicate values
items.answer: Because the inner loop is nested inside the outer loop, so comparisons scale with n * n
items.explanation_md: Nesting multiplies, not adds: for each of the n outer iterations, the inner loop can run up to n more times, giving roughly n * n = O(n^2) comparisons total.

items.prompt_md: You want to know how many operations an approach performs as the input size `n` grows large. Which approach determines this correctly?
options: Time the code once on a single input size and note the seconds elapsed
Count how the number of basic operations (comparisons, lookups, iterations) scales with n, then keep only the fastest-growing term
Count the number of lines in the function — more lines means slower growth
Assume every loop, nested or not, contributes O(n) to the total
items.answer: Count how the number of basic operations (comparisons, lookups, iterations) scales with n, then keep only the fastest-growing term
items.explanation_md: Big-O is about the shape of growth, not a stopwatch reading or a line count. Nested loops over the same n elements multiply their counts rather than add them, which is why counting operations (not timing or reading code length) is the reliable method.

items.prompt_md: How do the time complexities of the brute-force nested-loop version and the one-pass hash-map version of Two Sum compare?
options: Brute force is O(n), hash map is O(n^2)
Brute force is O(n^2), hash map is O(n)
items.answer: Brute force is O(n^2), hash map is O(n)
items.explanation_md: The brute-force version compares every pair (n * n work). The hash-map version replaces the inner loop with an O(1) lookup, so the single outer loop of n steps dominates: O(n) total.
